title: Controlled Vocabulary: Story Element Object Types
description: Canonical set of object types used across archetype element templates. Every object in an element template or instance must be assigned exactly one type. These types describe narrative function — why an object matters to the story — not its physical form.

object_types.definition: An object that enables the protagonist or antagonist to exert force, confront threats, or overcome physical obstacles.
object_types.structural_function: Enables action at Crisis and Trial nodes. Its acquisition or loss marks power shifts. May carry symbolic weight beyond utility.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Lightsaber (Hero's Journey), Sting (The Quest), The Murder Weapon (The Revenge), The Enchanted Sword (Overcoming the Monster)

object_types.definition: A protective or empowering object given by a mentor or earned through trial — carries personal or magical significance.
object_types.structural_function: Connects protagonist to mentor or source of power. Often received at Catalyst phase and critical at Crisis. Loss may signal vulnerability.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Ring of Power (The Quest), The Mentor's Gift (Hero's Journey), The Lucky Charm (Comedy), The Family Heirloom (Coming of Age)

object_types.definition: A text, letter, map, or recorded information that reveals truth, provides direction, or serves as evidence.
object_types.structural_function: Drives Revelation nodes. Its discovery or interpretation changes understanding. May be a map, letter, prophecy, or record.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Treasure Map (The Quest), The Confession Letter (Mystery Unveiled), The Prophecy (Overcoming the Monster), The Will (Rags to Riches)

object_types.definition: An object of great value — monetary, strategic, or symbolic — that motivates action or represents the story's prize.
object_types.structural_function: Serves as the explicit goal or reward. Its acquisition represents Resolution; its loss represents failure. Drives plot momentum.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Holy Grail (The Quest), The Fortune (Rags to Riches), The Crown (Rise and Fall), The Stolen Goods (The Revenge)

object_types.definition: An object whose specific nature matters less than the fact that everyone wants it — drives the plot through pursuit rather than inherent properties.
object_types.structural_function: Motivates character movement and conflict without requiring detailed explanation. The chase matters more than the thing.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Death Star Plans (Hero's Journey), The Briefcase (Thriller archetype patterns), The Secret Formula (Overcoming the Monster)

object_types.definition: An object that carries thematic weight — it represents an idea, value, or state of being rather than serving a practical function.
object_types.structural_function: Externalizes theme. Its state tracks the story's thematic arc (intact = hope, broken = loss, restored = redemption).
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Rose (Rebirth), The Mirror (The Transformation), The Wedding Ring (Comedy), The Crown (Tragedy)

object_types.definition: A practical object that solves specific problems — its value is utilitarian rather than symbolic or magical.
object_types.structural_function: Enables specific actions at Trial nodes. Unlike talismans, tools derive power from the user's skill, not inherent magic.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Lockpick (The Escape), The Disguise (The Transformation), The Climbing Rope (The Quest), The Vehicle (Voyage and Return)

object_types.definition: An object that unlocks access — to a place, a truth, a power, or a next phase of the story. Literal or figurative.
object_types.structural_function: Gates progression. Its possession determines whether the protagonist can advance past a threshold or into a stronghold.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Literal Key (The Escape), The Password (Mystery Unveiled), The Invitation (Rags to Riches), The Access Code (Overcoming the Monster)

object_types.definition: An object that contains, carries, or transports something of importance — may hold power, knowledge, or another entity.
object_types.structural_function: Controls access to a resource or truth. Opening, filling, or breaking the vessel is often a pivotal moment.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Ark of the Covenant (Hero's Journey), Pandora's Box (Tragedy), The Potion Bottle (Rebirth), The Ship (Voyage and Return)

object_types.definition: An object from a previous era or predecessor whose significance lies in its connection to history, legacy, or inherited power.
object_types.structural_function: Links the current story to a larger continuity. Carries the weight of prior events. Its discovery often triggers Revelation.
object_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Ancient Artifact (The Quest), The Ancestral Blade (Hero's Journey), The Old Photograph (Coming of Age), The Predecessor's Journal (Mystery Unveiled)
